prompt: I have an article titled 'Why VACUUM blocks during long transactions in Postgres'. Write the full distribution package: (1) a dek/subtitle of 25-50 words; (2) a meta description of 150-160 characters for SEO; (3) an Open Graph image concept described in one paragraph (what should the image show — be concrete). Save all three to package.md with clear Markdown headings (## Dek, ## Meta description, ## OG image). Don't ask follow-up questions.
assertions: The Dek section in package.md does NOT repeat the title — measured by: NO consecutive substring of 5+ words from the title appears in the dek. Specifically, the dek must NOT contain the exact phrase 'VACUUM blocks during long transactions' or 'Why VACUUM blocks' anywhere in the dek body. The skill explicitly teaches 'dek extends, doesn't repeat'; baseline often opens the dek by restating the title. Lowercased substring comparison; punctuation differences ignored.

The Meta description section in package.md has a character count between 140 and 165 characters inclusive (giving slight tolerance around the 150-160 band) AND contains AT LEAST 2 mechanism words connecting concrete deliverables — mechanism words: 'how', 'why', 'what', 'and', 'plus', 'including', 'because', 'so', '+'. Examples that pass: 'How dead tuples accumulate, why VACUUM stalls, and the alert that catches it'. Examples that fail: 'Learn about Postgres VACUUM' (too short, no mechanism), generic 200+ char marketing prose. Baseline tends to either overflow or write 60-100 char generic descriptions.

The OG image section in package.md describes a SPECIFIC non-generic visual concept. The description must NOT consist solely of any of these baseline-default placeholder ideas (case-insensitive): 'stock photo', 'a developer at a computer', 'a database server', 'the company logo', 'a generic image', 'a person typing', 'an abstract design'. The description MUST mention AT LEAST ONE of: rendered typography of the title, a specific chart/diagram concept tied to the article (e.g. timeline, transaction-state graph, before/after metric), a code/terminal-output visualization, or a specific contextual photograph (with subject named — not 'a developer' but 'a postgres terminal output' or 'a chart of dead-tuple growth over time'). The skill teaches that OG images extend the article's promise; generic stock images are explicitly named as anti-patterns.
